source.title: Cortical substrates for exploratory decisions in humans

confidence_rationale: fMRI dissociation in healthy humans: frontopolar cortex + intraparietal sulcus selectively active during exploratory decisions; striatum + vmPFC during exploitative. The paper introduces the standard computational characterisation of the explore-exploit dilemma that the rest of the field uses. Key R2 evidence: there is a distinct cortical substrate for exploration (not just the dopaminergic exploitation circuit). Confidence high but not maximal because fMRI cannot dissociate the curiosity-bias channel from the noise channel within the frontopolar BOLD signal.
failure_signatures: If REE has only an exploitation circuit (E3 score-aggregation + GoalState seeding) without a separate diversity-generation pathway, the model should fail to produce frontopolar-equivalent activation patterns during the explore phases of behaviour.

If diversity generation in REE is not separable from exploitation but emerges from noise alone (R1 noise-only verdict), then no substrate should map onto the frontopolar/vmPFC double dissociation Daw 2006 reports.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Frontopolar cortex and intraparietal sulcus are preferentially active during exploratory decisions; striatum and ventromedial prefrontal cortex are preferentially active during exploitative decisions. The two are dissociable substrates.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-065 should be architecturally distinct from the exploitation pathway already substrate-landed in REE (E3 score selection + SD-033a/b PFC bias channels + dACC adaptive control). Daw 2006 provides the dissociation evidence: separate brain substrates for exploration vs exploitation argue for a separate ARC-065 cluster, not an extension of an existing wanting/value-bias claim. R3 verdict: PROMOTE-TO-CLUSTER (separate slot warranted).
mapping.mapping_caveat: fMRI cannot resolve the within-frontopolar circuit dynamics (cell types, projection targets); the 'exploration substrate' is a regional functional dissociation, not a circuit-level mechanism. The paper does not directly test the structured-vs-noise question (R1) -- it identifies WHERE exploratory decisions are processed, not WHAT generates the decision noise itself.
mapping.source_context: fMRI four-armed bandit task in healthy adult humans; reinforcement-learning model (Kalman filter) used to classify each trial as exploratory or exploitative; Gatsby + UCL FIL groups.

confidence_components.notes: Nature venue, foundational paper for the explore-exploit fMRI literature. Mapping fidelity moderate-high: it cleanly demonstrates separate substrates for exploration vs exploitation, but does not directly tell us whether the exploration substrate operates continuously or triggered, or which sub-mechanism (curiosity / noise / both) it implements.
